files.SKILL.md:
# Working Agent Discipline

Your job: produce one artifact at the exact path the brief names. That artifact passing the brief's check is the only definition of done.

## Do these in order, every task

1. Read `/workspace/INSTRUCTION.md` if you have not yet.
2. Read every file the brief names by path. Batch independent reads in one turn.
3. Early in the session — before extensive exploration — write a first-draft of the deliverable at the exact path. Even if uncertain. A non-empty file at the right path scores partial; a missing file scores zero.
4. Run whatever check the brief defines (a test it names, a script it names, an interactive verification it describes). Read the failure surface.
5. Overwrite the deliverable to fix failures. Repeat until the check is green or you are nearing the end of the session.

If the session is nearly over, stop testing, ship the best draft you have, exit.

## Output format precision

The check usually byte-compares structured output. Match the brief's example shape exactly: same headers, same field order, same types, integers rendered as integers, no trailing whitespace, no extra blank lines, exact paths. `/app/run.py` is not `/app/main.py`. When the brief shows an example, the example is the spec.

## When the verifier runs in a separate environment

If the brief tells you the verifier "runs in a fresh container", "copies your script in", or "runs as root in a clean environment", your container differs from the verifier's. Do not waste iterations trying to execute the script locally to "see if it works" — local apt, permissions, and state will not match. Validate by reading the script back and running a syntax check (`bash -n` for shell, parse-only for Python). Trust the verifier environment.

## Read what is given

If the brief names a script, file, or directory, read it before guessing what it does. Artifacts are more reliable than intuition. When you must reproduce or work around an existing artifact's behaviour, study its source byte-by-byte — the structure of the artifact tells you exactly what it does, and equally what it does not.

## Iteration is overwriting the artifact

Refinement happens by re-writing the file, not by reasoning in chat. Read failure → overwrite the file → re-check.

## Reach for the established tool

Standard Unix and language toolchains include well-worn tools for inspection, recovery, and reproducible scripting. When the brief names a familiar shape — examining a binary, working on a damaged or unfamiliar file format, retracing recent state changes in version control, building a configuration that another process will run — there is usually a single established tool that does the job. Use it; do not reinvent.

## Boundary semantics

Inclusive vs exclusive at thresholds, the exact endpoints of any range, and the precise wording of "today" vs "from start of today" are part of the spec, not defaults. Read the brief; pick deliberately.

## Parsing with care

When counting tagged categories in text or extracting fields from structured filenames, anchor on the structural delimiter, not the bare word. Substring-only matches over-count because the same word appears in ordinary prose. Parsing structure beats grepping the whole.

## Things engineers carry as habits

- Data structures that grow as work proceeds need to release entries when the work is finished. An accumulator that never sheds is a leak in waiting.
- Per-call work that scales with internal state size quietly degrades from microseconds to seconds.
- A read-then-decide-then-write on shared mutable state is a race unless the whole sequence is held under one guard.
- Durations belong to a monotonic source; wall-clock arithmetic breaks the moment the clock is stepped.

## Tooling notes

For multi-line writes, prefer a file-write tool that handles content as bytes, or a language-level write. Heredocs and other shell-quoted forms can corrupt content with backticks, dollar signs, or triple quotes. For single-character edits, edit in place rather than rewriting the whole file.

## Stop conditions

Deliverable exists at the named path AND the check is green = done. A prose summary in chat is not the deliverable. A small landed file beats a thoughtful draft that did not get written.

## Next action

If you have not yet read `/workspace/INSTRUCTION.md`, your next tool call is to read it.
